title: Share of GDP per capita spent by the government on each primary school student vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: GDP per capita data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Share of GDP per capita spent by the government on each primary school student vs. GDP per capita, 2008

GDP per capita data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps government expenditure per primary school student as a percentage of GDP per capita against national GDP per capita for 2008. The vertical axis measures spending intensity from 0% to over 40%, while the horizontal axis plots GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices on a logarithmic scale ranging from $2,000 to $100,000. Points are color-coded by region: North America (light orange), South America (maroon), Africa (purple), Europe (blue), Asia (teal), and Oceania (light blue). The data shows a wide dispersion of spending priorities across income levels. Serbia sits at the highest visible point on the vertical axis, exceeding 40% of GDP per capita, followed by Djibouti and Albania in the 30% to 40% range. In contrast, Kazakhstan and Rwanda appear near the bottom of the chart, with spending shares below 5%. On the horizontal axis, countries like Burundi and the Democratic Republic of Congo cluster at the lower end near $2,000, while Bermuda and Austria extend toward the $100,000 mark. A distinct cluster of European nations, including Cyprus, Latvia, and Estonia, occupies the middle-to-upper right section, showing spending shares between 20% and 30% alongside higher GDP figures. Conversely, several Asian and African nations such as Cambodia, Guinea, and Niger group in the lower-left quadrant with lower GDP and spending shares generally under 15%. The distribution reveals no single linear trend; instead, countries with low GDP per capita display both the lowest and some of the highest relative spending shares, while high-income nations tend to cluster in the 15% to 25% band.
